context.description: A philosophical book page argues that symbolic reality shapes behavior more strongly than factual reality, using propaganda, politics, and archetypes as examples. It emphasizes how inner symbolic filters influence perception and choices. A comparison of two equally capable people illustrates how different career paths lead to different worldviews.
normalization_notes: Normalized category and subcategory to the closest supported body-content theoretical discussion.

rows.paragraph_text: The symbolic world can shape behavior and make people act against their explicit expectations. Propaganda and politics work by influencing symbolic reality, while archetypes may operate in a similar way through the inner world. Perception is filtered by symbolic priorities before factual interpretation.

Two equally capable people can understand the world differently because their life paths shape different symbolic frameworks. The example contrasts a scientific career with psychology to show how worldview is organized before facts are processed.

content.free_text: book_page book_body_content theoretical_discussion Book page on symbolic reality, propaganda, archetypes, and perception filters in worldview formation.
